From: Smith, Claire M <claire.smith@imperial.ac.uk>
Sent: 24 January 2024 13:20
To: Neurotact UK <yaqixie@neurotact.uk>
Cc: susansmith@qmul.ac.uk <susansmith@qmul.ac.uk>; T.HUSSAIN@QMUL.AC.UK
<t.hussain@qmul.ac.uk>; Smith, Claire M <claire.smith@imperial.ac.uk>
Subject: RE: RSS request IC00021 - availability for a meePng with the RSS CTU team
Dear Yaki
Thank you for taking the Pme to discuss your research plans with the RSS team from Imperial College
and Partners (me, Tahera and Susan) last week. And thanks to Susan for providing the links during
our discussion. They also appear below for convenience.
Here are a few notes from that discussion:
• It's important to jusHfy why the research is needed:
What is the clinical problem? What is the scale of the problem? How does it impact the NHS / the
paPents? How will your technology address the problem? How will you measure improvement?
• Build your team
NIHR will be looking for a mulPdisciplinary team to support the work. We recommend as a minimum
geeng a clinician on board. Consider approaching the Medtech and In vitro diagnosPcs Co-
operaPves (MICs). hXps://www.nihr.ac.uk/partners-and-industry/industry/access-to-experPse/

medtech.htm. MICs can help you to build a mulP-disciplinary team that will work collaboraPvely with
you to ensure you generate the evidence you need to support adopPon of your medical device,
digital technology or diagnosPc in the NHS. The NIHR Devices 4 Dignity MIC – Delivering innovaPve
technology soluPons to support people with long-term condiPons, preserving their dignity and
independence (devicesfordignity.org.uk) might be parPcularly relevant.
• AI as a Medical Device
You menPoned that your device is currently class I (with no UKCA mark as yet) but once the AI
component is added then it becomes Class 1a. RegulaPon in this area is challenging and will conPnue
to evolve. Here is a link to a guidance document that you may find useful The RegulaPon of ArPficial
Intelligence as a Medical Device (publishing.service.gov.uk)
• Planning a feasibility study
And another link you may find helpful: Guidance on applying for feasibility studies | NIHR
• Focus of i4i Connect applicaHon
Trying to deliver a feasibility study in 12 months and a budget capped at £150k will be very
challenging. Also it may be too soon to embark on a feasibility study if you only have pilot data on 11
volunteers.
Consider changing the focus of the i4i applicaPon to include several work packages, for example (a)
PPI work including the workshops with paPents, family members and carers that you already have in
mind, (b) clinician engagement / forming collaboraPons (see comment above about building the
team) and (c) understanding the regulatory landscape (see comment above about AI as a MD).
• When to engage a Clinical Trials Unit
CTUs offer different levels of support with feasibility studies. Some CTUs will collaborate in full, some
will offer light touch support, e.g. operaPons / staPsPcs advisory roles and a database and some
CTUs don't support feasibility studies. The decision may in some cases be driven by budget
restricPons, for example the Research for PaPent Benefit funding stream Research for PaPent Benefit
| NIHR caps the budget for feasibility studies at £300k. I suggest that when you are ready to start
planning the feasibility study you make a new approach to the RSS to request advice and explore the
opportuniPes to collaborate with a CTU.
We wish you well with the next stage in your product development.
Kind regards
Claire
Claire Smith PhD
Business Development & OperaHons Manager – Clinical Research
Imperial Clinical Trials Unit
Please note my normal working days are Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday (am), Thursday.

Application Context: Applicant plans PPI workshops with patients, family members, and carers, supporting engagement with people and communities.

sub_criteria.name: Common Characteristics of Good Applications
sub_criteria.definition: The application demonstrates characteristics commonly found in strong grant applications.

signals.signal_text: Clear research vision and career trajectory
Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant demonstrates engagement with the RSS CTU team for guidance on team building and regulation, indicating some institutional support (g.1.d). There is a nascent research vision focused on pain management technology (g.1.a, g.1.b).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Feedback explicitly states the applicant needs to justify the clinical problem and scale, suggesting the research vision lacks sufficient clarity and justification (g.1.a). No specific training plan is mentioned (g.1.c). The application appears to be in a pre-submission consultation phase rather than a finalized proposal with a clear independent direction (g.1.b).
sub_criteria.rationale: Feedback explicitly states the applicant needs to justify the clinical problem and scale, suggesting the research vision lacks sufficient clarity and justification (g.1.a). No specific training plan is mentioned (g.1.c). The application appears to be in a pre-submission consultation phase rather than a finalized proposal with a clear independent direction (g.1.b).

sub_criteria.definition: The applicant clearly articulates why this specific award is the right next step in their career.
sub_criteria.group_name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

signals.signal_text: Explain why this award is the right next step in your career (not just another project grant)
What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

sub_criteria.pros: No evidence found in the provided text.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The text consists of email correspondence regarding a meeting and feedback, not the actual grant application. There is no articulation of why this specific award is the right next step, the difference it would make, or how it builds on experience.
sub_criteria.rationale: The text consists of email correspondence regarding a meeting and feedback, not the actual grant application. There is no articulation of why this specific award is the right next step, the difference it would make, or how it builds on experience.

sub_criteria.name: Relevant Research Experience and Training
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrates prior research experience and/or training that prepares the applicant to successfully undertake the proposed award in applied health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of previous projects, research methods, or techniques relevant to the proposed study
Evidence of formal training, courses, or certifications
Alignment between prior experience and skills required for the proposed project

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant mentions having pilot data on 11 volunteers, indicating some prior research activity (g.3.a).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No formal training, courses, or certifications are mentioned (g.3.b). The alignment between prior experience and the proposed project is weak, as feedback suggests the current pilot data is insufficient for the proposed feasibility study scope (g.3.c).
sub_criteria.rationale: No formal training, courses, or certifications are mentioned (g.3.b). The alignment between prior experience and the proposed project is weak, as feedback suggests the current pilot data is insufficient for the proposed feasibility study scope (g.3.c).

Subject: RE: RSS request IC00021 - availability for a meePng with the RSS CTU team
Dear Yaki
Thank you for taking the Pme to discuss your research plans with the RSS team from Imperial College
and Partners (me, Tahera and Susan) last week. And thanks to Susan for providing the links during
our discussion. They also appear below for convenience.
Here are a few notes from that discussion:
• It's important to jusHfy why the research is needed:
What is the clinical problem? What is the scale of the problem? How does it impact the NHS / the
paPents? How will your technology address the problem? How will you measure improvement?
• Build your team
NIHR will be looking for a mulPdisciplinary team to support the work. We recommend as a minimum
geeng a clinician on board. Consider approaching the Medtech and In vitro diagnosPcs Co-
operaPves (MICs). hXps://www.nihr.ac.uk/partners-and-industry/industry/access-to-experPse/

medtech.htm. MICs can help you to build a mulP-disciplinary team that will work collaboraPvely with
you to ensure you generate the evidence you need to support adopPon of your medical device,
digital technology or diagnosPc in the NHS. The NIHR Devices 4 Dignity MIC – Delivering innovaPve
technology soluPons to support people with long-term condiPons, preserving their dignity and
independence (devicesfordignity.org.uk) might be parPcularly relevant.
• AI as a Medical Device
You menPoned that your device is currently class I (with no UKCA mark as yet) but once the AI
component is added then it becomes Class 1a. RegulaPon in this area is challenging and will conPnue
to evolve. Here is a link to a guidance document that you may find useful The RegulaPon of ArPficial
Intelligence as a Medical Device (publishing.service.gov.uk)
• Planning a feasibility study
And another link you may find helpful: Guidance on applying for feasibility studies | NIHR
• Focus of i4i Connect applicaHon
Trying to deliver a feasibility study in 12 months and a budget capped at £150k will be very
challenging. Also it may be too soon to embark on a feasibility study if you only have pilot data on 11
volunteers.

Cc: susansmith@qmul.ac.uk <susansmith@qmul.ac.uk>; T.HUSSAIN@QMUL.AC.UK
<t.hussain@qmul.ac.uk>; Smith, Claire M <claire.smith@imperial.ac.uk>
Subject: RE: RSS request IC00021 - availability for a meePng with the RSS CTU team
Dear Yaki
Thank you for taking the Pme to discuss your research plans with the RSS team from Imperial College
and Partners (me, Tahera and Susan) last week. And thanks to Susan for providing the links during
our discussion. They also appear below for convenience.
Here are a few notes from that discussion:
• It's important to jusHfy why the research is needed:
What is the clinical problem? What is the scale of the problem? How does it impact the NHS / the
paPents? How will your technology address the problem? How will you measure improvement?
• Build your team
NIHR will be looking for a mulPdisciplinary team to support the work. We recommend as a minimum
geeng a clinician on board. Consider approaching the Medtech and In vitro diagnosPcs Co-
operaPves (MICs). hXps://www.nihr.ac.uk/partners-and-industry/industry/access-to-experPse/

medtech.htm. MICs can help you to build a mulP-disciplinary team that will work collaboraPvely with
you to ensure you generate the evidence you need to support adopPon of your medical device,
digital technology or diagnosPc in the NHS. The NIHR Devices 4 Dignity MIC – Delivering innovaPve
technology soluPons to support people with long-term condiPons, preserving their dignity and
independence (devicesfordignity.org.uk) might be parPcularly relevant.
• AI as a Medical Device
You menPoned that your device is currently class I (with no UKCA mark as yet) but once the AI
component is added then it becomes Class 1a. RegulaPon in this area is challenging and will conPnue
to evolve. Here is a link to a guidance document that you may find useful The RegulaPon of ArPficial
Intelligence as a Medical Device (publishing.service.gov.uk)
• Planning a feasibility study
And another link you may find helpful: Guidance on applying for feasibility studies | NIHR
• Focus of i4i Connect applicaHon
Trying to deliver a feasibility study in 12 months and a budget capped at £150k will be very
challenging. Also it may be too soon to embark on a feasibility study if you only have pilot data on 11
volunteers.
Consider changing the focus of the i4i applicaPon to include several work packages, for example (a)
PPI work including the workshops with paPents, family members and carers that you already have in
mind, (b) clinician engagement / forming collaboraPons (see comment above about building the
team) and (c) understanding the regulatory landscape (see comment above about AI as a MD).
• When to engage a Clinical Trials Unit
CTUs offer different levels of support with feasibility studies. Some CTUs will collaborate in full, some
will offer light touch support, e.g. operaPons / staPsPcs advisory roles and a database and some
CTUs don't support feasibility studies. The decision may in some cases be driven by budget
restricPons, for example the Research for PaPent Benefit funding stream Research for PaPent Benefit
| NIHR caps the budget for feasibility studies at £300k. I suggest that when you are ready to start
planning the feasibility study you make a new approach to the RSS to request advice and explore the
opportuniPes to collaborate with a CTU.
We wish you well with the next stage in your product development.
Kind regards
Claire
Claire Smith PhD

Imperial Clinical Trials Unit

Subject: RE: RSS request IC00021 - availability for a meePng with the RSS CTU team
Dear Yaki
Thank you for taking the Pme to discuss your research plans with the RSS team from Imperial College
and Partners (me, Tahera and Susan) last week. And thanks to Susan for providing the links during
our discussion. They also appear below for convenience.
Here are a few notes from that discussion:
• It's important to jusHfy why the research is needed:
What is the clinical problem? What is the scale of the problem? How does it impact the NHS / the
paPents? How will your technology address the problem? How will you measure improvement?
• Build your team
NIHR will be looking for a mulPdisciplinary team to support the work. We recommend as a minimum
geeng a clinician on board. Consider approaching the Medtech and In vitro diagnosPcs Co-
operaPves (MICs). hXps://www.nihr.ac.uk/partners-and-industry/industry/access-to-experPse/

medtech.htm. MICs can help you to build a mulP-disciplinary team that will work collaboraPvely with
you to ensure you generate the evidence you need to support adopPon of your medical device,
digital technology or diagnosPc in the NHS. The NIHR Devices 4 Dignity MIC – Delivering innovaPve
technology soluPons to support people with long-term condiPons, preserving their dignity and
independence (devicesfordignity.org.uk) might be parPcularly relevant.
• AI as a Medical Device
You menPoned that your device is currently class I (with no UKCA mark as yet) but once the AI
component is added then it becomes Class 1a. RegulaPon in this area is challenging and will conPnue
to evolve. Here is a link to a guidance document that you may find useful The RegulaPon of ArPficial
Intelligence as a Medical Device (publishing.service.gov.uk)
• Planning a feasibility study
And another link you may find helpful: Guidance on applying for feasibility studies | NIHR
• Focus of i4i Connect applicaHon
Trying to deliver a feasibility study in 12 months and a budget capped at £150k will be very
challenging. Also it may be too soon to embark on a feasibility study if you only have pilot data on 11
volunteers.
Consider changing the focus of the i4i applicaPon to include several work packages, for example (a)
PPI work including the workshops with paPents, family members and carers that you already have in
mind, (b) clinician engagement / forming collaboraPons (see comment above about building the
team) and (c) understanding the regulatory landscape (see comment above about AI as a MD).
• When to engage a Clinical Trials Unit
CTUs offer different levels of support with feasibility studies. Some CTUs will collaborate in full, some
will offer light touch support, e.g. operaPons / staPsPcs advisory roles and a database and some
CTUs don't support feasibility studies. The decision may in some cases be driven by budget
restricPons, for example the Research for PaPent Benefit funding stream Research for PaPent Benefit
| NIHR caps the budget for feasibility studies at £300k. I suggest that when you are ready to start
planning the feasibility study you make a new approach to the RSS to request advice and explore the
opportuniPes to collaborate with a CTU.
We wish you well with the next stage in your product development.
Kind regards
